sections.directions:
YOUR TASK: Write an argumentative essay in response to the Key Question.

The various perspectives are presented to provide additional context for the issue and to give you ideas that you can react to or incorporate into your argument as you develop it. They also serve as a model for the types of arguments that various stakeholders are making as they debate the issue raised in the Key Question.

Your essay must directly address ideas from one or more of the perspectives presented in the task. You do not need to address each of the perspectives—use your own judgment in deciding which ideas to address (and how many), based on what you believe will be most effective for developing your argument.

Use the ideas generated during your prewriting analysis to help you create and develop your argument as you see fit. You may incorporate any knowledge or experiences you might have regarding this issue, your own values, and your critical evaluation of the arguments and ideas contained in the other perspectives. Your position may be in full agreement with any of the perspectives, in partial agreement, or completely different. Whatever the case, your position should be supported with logical arguments and detailed, persuasive examples.

items.stimulusText:
Isolation vs. Intervention

The basic principle of state sovereignty is that each national government retains authority over its own, and only its own, territory. Nevertheless, national governments often devote significant resources to addressing problems outside their territorial boundaries. For example, the United States government spent approximately $52.4 billion (about 2 percent of its overall budget) in humanitarian and military aid to foreign nations in 2021 alone; the Canadian and Chinese governments also spent billions on foreign aid that same year. Additionally, national governments often impose economic or diplomatic sanctions against other national governments in response to real or perceived human rights abuses, threats to global peace, or violations of international law. Still, despite the established, commonplace nature of such practices, national governments’ involvement in foreign problems is frequently controversial. For example, U.S. polls consistently show that foreign aid is the federal budget item that the populace is most likely to support reducing or eliminating entirely (although specific aid initiatives can be widely popular).

KEY QUESTION: To what extent, if any, should any national government attempt to address problems that are occurring wholly outside the borders of its own country?

Read and carefully consider the arguments presented in the following perspectives. Each suggests a particular way of thinking about the issue captured in the Key Question.

Perspective 1 — an excerpt from an essay in a philosophy journal

Relationships are particular. We rightly expect parents to find their own children more loveable and valuable than any other children—not in any objective sense, but more loveable and valuable to them. This is what being a parent means. The same is true for national governments. We rightly expect national governments to value their citizens, and their citizens’ interests, above all other peoples and their interests. The nature of the relationship between citizens and their government demands it: National governments' primary purpose is to uphold the collective good of their respective populations. Aid, embargoes, and military interventions all require the use of scarce energy and resources that could instead benefit a nation's own people. So when national governments choose to involve themselves in foreign entanglements, they are deciding to promote some foreign interest at the expense of their own citizens. This is a fundamental betrayal.

Perspective 2 — an excerpt from a speech by a national politician

I like to invoke the Spider-Man principle: With great power comes great responsibility. The national governments that have the largest economies are the most powerful entities in the world. They control the most resources—money, medical supplies, fuel, technology, and so on. They can muster the largest numbers of people. And usually, they have the greatest authority on the international stage. That power imposes a responsibility on every national government—including ours—to be a force for good in the world. This requires taking positive action. Refraining from abusing our power isn’t enough. We must step in when and where we can: responding to natural disasters and famines, promoting education and economic development, and yes, even taking action to end or prevent wide-scale abuses by other governments. 

I know it’s tempting to ask, "But what do we get from all that?" It doesn’t matter. That responsibility to do good exists even if the answer is, "Nothing at all."

Perspective 3 — an excerpt from a post on a foreign policy blog

We live in a world of multinational businesses, international trade, and global research. Millions of people carry dual citizenship. Millions more live and work outside their home countries. People can hop continents in a mere matter of hours. Ideas can spread across the world in seconds. So separating our nation’s problems from other nations’ problems according to which side of a national boundary they fall on is simply shortsighted. Governments should not count on currently local problems staying local. An outbreak of, say, mad cow disease in Australia can quickly become a crisis for the beef market in Ireland. We should consider any such problem to be our nation’s problem if it is likely to affect any of our nation’s citizens.

Perspective 4 — an excerpt from a lecture about international development

Foreign aid is nothing more than modern colonialism. The concept is clearly paternalistic: Powerful nations feel both compelled and entitled to support their weaker counterparts, who are presumed to be incapable of taking care of themselves. But even putting aside the paternalism, the aid is not benign. First, it always comes with strings attached. Donor governments generally insist that recipient governments take certain actions—like changing policies disapproved of by the donor governments—under the threat of withdrawal of future aid. Second, the aid itself can be culturally imperialistic. Consider civil-society aid. Here, donor governments provide funding and other resources to promote specific norms among the population of a recipient nation, such as free-market capitalism or centrally planned, socialist economies. These norms are, of course, the norms of the donor governments themselves. But this also means coercing the recipient nations into abandoning any of their own traditional forms of government and social organization that cannot be made to fit into the donor governments’ mold.

Prewriting Analysis: Generating Ideas for Your Argumentative Essay

Spend the remainder of your prewriting time working through the prewriting questions presented below. These questions are intended to help you think critically about the issue and generate productive ideas for your essay. Record your thoughts in your digital scratch paper by making notes or lists, or by writing short answers to the questions.

Your notes in response to the prewriting questions will still be available to you while you write your essay, but they will not be evaluated or included as part of your essay. These questions are strictly provided to help guide your analysis of the perspectives and to help you develop your own argument in response to the Key Question.

Prewriting Questions

  • Which perspective(s) do you find most compelling?
  • What relevant insights do you see in the perspective(s)?
  • What principles or values do you see at work in the perspective(s)?
  • What strengths and weaknesses can you find in the perspective(s)?

Your argument should incorporate or address ideas from at least one of the perspectives provided. In addition, your own knowledge, experiences, and personal values can be valid sources of evidence, and you can include these in your essay:

  • What knowledge do you already have about this issue? Consider information you have read or heard, including things you’ve learned at home or school, etc.
  • What values influence your position on this issue? Consider your worldview or belief system, as well as any guiding principles or convictions you hold.
  • What experiences do you have that might be relevant to this issue? Consider any personal experience you might have with this or similar issues, or other relevant lessons learned from your own life.

description: Prepare for the new LSAT Argumentative Writing with this practice prompt and task format if you plan to take LSAT Writing in August 2024 or after. This practice writing prompt experience mirrors the same format as the actual LSAT Argumentative Writing.  (Note that some prompts will have three perspectives whereas others will have four, but they are all roughly the same length.)
directions:
The following task is designed to measure your ability to write an argumentative essay. You will be presented with a debatable issue (identified in the Key Question), along with different perspectives that provide additional context for the issue by introducing various claims that have been made within that debate. You will then write an argumentative essay that takes a position within this ongoing conversation, while addressing at least one of the arguments and ideas presented in the other perspectives.

Before writing your essay, you will have some time for prewriting analysis. An on-screen timer will display the amount of time you have remaining for prewriting analysis. Use this time to read and analyze the perspectives in this conversation and reflect on the questions we’ve included to help you generate ideas for your essay.

Your prewriting analysis timer will start when you select the "Begin" button above. You can move forward to the essay-writing screen after 5 minutes have elapsed, if you choose to. After you move on from the prewriting analysis, a new on-screen timer will begin. You will then have the amount of time displayed on this timer in which to write your essay. Your essay will be submitted automatically when the time has fully elapsed. If you finish writing your essay before the time has elapsed, you may select "Submit" and then follow the directions that appear on your screen.

No specialized background knowledge is required or expected for this writing exercise. How well you write is much more important than how much you write.

A strong response will:

  • clearly state the thesis of your argument
  • develop your thesis throughout your essay by connecting specific examples to your overall thesis and explaining their relevance to the thesis with clear reasoning
  • address the complexities and implications of your essay’s position (for example, by identifying and addressing one or more potential counterarguments)

Your essay should demonstrate your ability to:

  • clearly state a position on the issue and analyze the relationship between that position and one or more of the other perspectives
  • develop and support ideas with reasoning and examples
  • organize ideas clearly and logically
  • communicate ideas using clear and effectively chosen language
